instruction: Apply WCAG principles to evaluate a web form for perceivability, operability, understandability, and robustness.
output:
<think>
WCAG has four principles. Perceivable: can all users sense the content? Check: every input has a visible label AND an associated label element (not just placeholder text which disappears). Images of text have alt text. Color is not the only way to convey information (error fields also have an icon, not just red border). Operable: can users interact with everything? Check: all controls reachable via keyboard Tab, focus order matches visual order, no keyboard traps (modal dialogs must trap focus inside but release on close). Understandable: can users comprehend the interface? Check: error messages are specific (not just "invalid input" but "email must contain @"), form fields have help text, required fields are clearly marked. Robust: does it work across assistive technologies? Check: semantic HTML (button not div), ARIA roles where needed, custom components implement WAI-ARIA patterns.
</think>

instruction: Design a color system that maintains WCAG AA contrast ratios while supporting both light and dark themes.
output:
<think>
WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18px+ or 14px+ bold). Design approach: define semantic color tokens (text-primary, text-secondary, surface, surface-elevated) rather than literal colors. For light theme: text-primary might be #1a1a1a on #ffffff background (contrast 17.4:1, passes AAA). For dark theme: text-primary might be #e0e0e0 on #121212 (contrast 13.2:1, passes AAA). Common mistake: dark theme is NOT just inverting colors — pure white text on pure black is actually harder to read than slightly muted combinations. Test every foreground/background combination. Automated tools: axe-core for automated checks, Lighthouse accessibility audit, Stark plugin for design tools. Interactive elements need additional contrast requirements for their focus/hover/active states.
</think>

instruction: Design keyboard navigation for a complex dashboard with multiple interactive widgets and modal dialogs.
output:
<think>
Keyboard navigation principles: (1) Every interactive element must be reachable via Tab. Tab order follows the visual reading order (top-to-bottom, left-to-right in LTR layouts). (2) Custom widgets implement ARIA keyboard patterns: tab panels use arrow keys to switch tabs, menus use arrow keys to navigate items, trees use arrow keys for expand/collapse. (3) Focus management: when a modal opens, focus moves to the first focusable element inside and is trapped (Tab cycles within the modal). When the modal closes, focus returns to the element that triggered it. (4) Skip navigation: a hidden "Skip to main content" link appears on first Tab press, allowing keyboard users to bypass repetitive navigation menus. (5) Focus indicators: never remove outline:none globally. Use :focus-visible to show focus rings only for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks. Custom focus indicators should have sufficient contrast (3:1 minimum).
</think>

instruction: Design screen reader-compatible alternatives to data visualizations like charts and graphs.
output:
<think>
Screen readers cannot interpret visual charts. Strategy: provide equivalent information in accessible formats alongside the visual. (1) Text summary: below each chart, include a paragraph describing the key insights ("Revenue increased 15% from Q1 to Q2, with the largest growth in the enterprise segment"). (2) Data table: provide the underlying data in a properly structured HTML table with headers (th elements), scope attributes, and caption. The table conveys the same information as the chart. (3) ARIA live regions: for dynamic dashboards, use aria-live to announce updates ("New data point: March revenue $1.2M, up 3% from February"). (4) Downloadable data: offer CSV export so users can analyze data in their preferred tools. (5) Progressive enhancement: build the data table first (accessible baseline), then layer the visual chart on top as an enhancement. Both render from the same data source to stay in sync.
</think>
